text: The surgeon performs an appendectomy by separating the appendix from surrounding tissues using bipolar cautery and scissors. The mesentery is dissected, and the base of the appendix is cleaned. A suture ligation with zero silk is applied twice before the appendix is divided and removed.

The surgeon performs an appendectomy, using bipolar cautery and scissors to dissect through the mesentery and clean the base of the appendix. A suture ligation with zero silk is applied to secure the appendix.

The surgeon divides and removes the appendix, then proceeds with a medial to lateral dissection. The lateral fibrotic and inflammatory adhesions are left attached to the sidewall, serving as natural retraction, while the retroperitoneal plane remains unaffected by inflammation.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, leaving lateral fibrotic and inflammatory adhesions attached to the sidewall for natural retraction. The retroperitoneal plane remains unviolated by inflammation. The superior hemorrhoidal vessel is isolated and cleaned for safe sealing. The ureter is identified and carefully dissected using ICG mode to avoid injury. A sponge is placed to keep the ureter posterior during lateral dissection. The splenic flexure is mobilized for anastomosis length, and the medial dissection is joined with the lateral dissection.

The surgeon begins dissection beneath the superior hemorrhoidal vessel, identifying a large inflamed lymph node and locating the ureter. The ureter is involved in the inflammatory reaction, and the surgeon uses ICG to aid in meticulous dissection. The superior hemorrhoidal vessel is isolated and cleaned to ensure it can be safely taken with a vessel sealer.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, maintaining the retroperitoneal plane intact. A sponge is placed underneath to keep the ureter posterior. The surgeon proceeds with lateral dissection, aiming to join the previous medial dissection, and moves towards the left pelvic brim where the tissue is notably adherent.

The surgeon attempts to dissect down towards the left pelvic brim, encountering significant adhesions due to chronic inflammation. Using blunt dissection, the surgeon navigates the challenging area to reach the true plane. The robotic arms provide torque for retraction, aiding in freeing the right fallopian tube and ovary. The sigmoid colon is visibly stuck deep in the pelvis, adherent to the back of the uterus and cervix.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a suction device to navigate through chronic inflammation in the pelvic region. The robotic arms provide torque for retraction, aiding in freeing the right tube and ovary. The sigmoid colon is visibly adherent to the back of the uterus and cervix, presenting challenges in dissection. The procedure becomes bloody at certain points, and the surgeon suspends the uterus after freeing it.

The surgeon uses a suction device for blunt dissection deep in the pelvis, encountering some bleeding. The uterus is suspended to free the first robotic arm for retraction. The surgeon identifies granulation tissue associated with a fistula and finds a plane lateral and inferior to the left fallopian tube and ovary. The left tube and ovary are seen adhered to the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon. The surgeon uses the suction device to encircle the fallopian tube, preparing to divide and remove the tube and ovary en bloc with the colon, facilitating further dissection in the left pelvic brim.

The surgeon uses the first robotic arm for retraction while working deep in the pelvis. Granulation tissue associated with a fistula is visible. The surgeon identifies a plane lateral and inferior to the left fallopian tube and ovary, which are adhered to the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon. The suction device is maneuvered around the fallopian tube, and the surgeon prepares to divide and remove the tube and ovary en bloc with the colon.

The surgeon opens up the left pelvic brim, facilitating dissection by cleaning the area with suction. The colon is visibly adhered to the anterior peritoneal reflection, and the surgeon separates the distal sigmoid loop from the peritoneal reflection, revealing the effects of a deep abscess.

The surgeon uses suction to clean the surgical field deep in the pelvis, where the colon is adhered to the anterior peritoneal reflection due to a previous abscess. The distal sigmoid loop is separated from the peritoneal reflection using scissors, with careful attention to avoid involving the left pelvic sidewall or internal iliac branches. Blunt dissection is performed to minimize thermal spread to the ureter, and a ligature is used to complete the dissection. Minor bleeding from the sidewall is controlled with Surgicil.

The surgeon uses scissors to carve through the area of concern near the ureter, attempting to avoid the left pelvic sidewall and branches of the internal iliac. Blunt dissection is performed to minimize thermal spread to the ureter, followed by ligature application. Minor bleeding from the sidewall is addressed with Surgicil.

The surgeon has successfully removed the lupus sigmoid from the pelvis and is cleaning the distal rectal mesentery to prepare for transection. The transection is positioned lower than usual for a sigmoid resection, just above the peritoneal reflection, due to scarring and disease damage. The proximal mesentery is also being addressed, with the splenic flexure previously mobilized for additional length. The surgeon marks the division point with cautery after checking perfusion with ICG.

The surgeon has successfully mobilized the lupus sigmoid from the pelvis and is now cleaning the distal rectal mesentery to prepare for transection. The transection is positioned lower than typical for a sigmoid resection, just above the peritoneal reflection, due to scarring and disease. The proximal mesentery is also being addressed, with previous mobilization of the splenic flexure for adequate length. The surgeon marks the division point with cautery after checking perfusion with ICG.
The surgeon prepares to divide the distal rectum and performs an intracorporeal hand-sewn anastomosis, avoiding transrectal extraction due to concerns about potential tearing of the distal rectum.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal hand-sewn anastomosis, suturing the two posterior staple lines together to form an outer posterior layer. Perfusion is checked, followed by the creation of a proctotomy and collotomy using scissors. The inner back row is sutured with a 3-0 V-Loc suture, starting in the middle and extending around the left side.

The surgeon sutures the two posterior staple lines together to form an outer posterior layer, checks perfusion, and performs a proctotomy and collotomy using scissors. The inner back row is sutured with a 3-0 V-Loc suture, starting in the middle and moving around the left side of the anastomosis. The needle is turned for better angle, and suturing continues towards the right side, forming an inner anterior row with a baseball stitch. The sutures are tied together for additional security, and the original 3-0 V-Loc suture is used to secure the anterior suture line.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis deep in the pelvis, suturing around the left side of the bowel edges, which appear irregular. The needle is turned to facilitate stitching from the middle towards the right side, followed by a baseball stitch over the top to complete the inner anterior row.

The surgeon ties two sutures together for additional security, using a three OB lock to suture the anterior row of the anastomosis. The surgeon ensures the entire corner is closed by suturing out to the silk, confirming the anastomosis is secure. A leak test is performed using a 19mm proctoscope to verify patency, followed by a vaginoscopy to check for defects, concluding with no bubbles observed.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis by suturing out to the silk for additional security, ensuring the corner is closed. A leak test is performed using a 19mm proctoscope through the anastomosis to confirm patency. A vaginoscopy is conducted, showing no defects in the vagina, with good distension and no bubbles observed.
